title: PagerDuty, Opsgenie, VictorOps, and Splunk On-Call Incident Lifecycle Tracking, On-Call Acknowledgement SLA, and MTTA/MTTR Trending Across Tools

premiumApps.name: Splunk ITSI
premiumApps.note: optional — compare episode start times to paging incident_id timelines without duplicating UC-13.3.5 storm detection

dataSources: `index=observability` (or `index=pagerduty`, `index=opsgenie`, `index=victorops`) with `sourcetype=pagerduty:incident`, `sourcetype=pagerduty:webhook`, `sourcetype=opsgenie:alert`, `sourcetype=opsgenie:notification`, `sourcetype=splunk_oncall:incident`, `sourcetype=victorops:incident`; accelerated `datamodel=Alerts` for Splunk-native correlation via `tstats`; lookups `oncall_responder_shift_roster.csv` and `escalation_policy_ground_truth.csv`.
app: Splunk Enterprise or Splunk Cloud with Splunk Add-on for PagerDuty (Splunkbase), Splunk Add-on for Opsgenie (Splunkbase), HTTP Event Collector or modular inputs for Splunk On-Call exports, plus CIM Alerts mapping for saved-search and correlation-search telemetry
description: Detects on-call paging lifecycle drift across PagerDuty, Opsgenie, Splunk On-Call, and VictorOps incident streams by normalizing incident_id, service_name, urgency, escalation_level, created_at, acknowledged_at, and resolved_at fields, then computing acknowledgement interval service levels, mean resolve durations, acknowledgement P95 tail latency, escalation-past-primary rates, noisy-service rankings, auto-resolve-before-ack ratios, and Splunk-native alert reconciliation hints against accelerated CIM Alerts activity.
value: Reliability and incident-management leaders see whether rotations actually meet acknowledgement promises, where resolve times concentrate by service and priority, which teams absorb unfair paging load, and when automation clears faults before humans react, so staffing, escalation design, and source-of-truth arguments shrink instead of festering across bridges.
grandmaExplanation: We track urgent calls for help from the tools that page people at night: how fast someone answers, how long fixes take, which services ring the phone most, and when problems heal on their own, so managers can fix unfair shifts and noisy services without guessing who was slow.
implementation: Ingest PagerDuty, Opsgenie, Splunk On-Call or VictorOps lanes plus accelerated CIM Alerts into observability indexes; normalize incident and timestamp fields; keep oncall_responder_shift_roster.csv and escalation_policy_ground_truth.csv current; schedule observability_uc_13_3_3_oncall_mtta_mttr_reconciliation every fifteen minutes with earliest=-24h@h; route SLA breach rows to platform reliability owners; archive weekly CSV evidence to your governance index.
detailedImplementation:
Step 1 — Prerequisites

Head of Platform owns this control jointly with incident response leadership, the teams that administer PagerDuty services and escalation policies, Atlassian Opsgenie teams and routing rules, and Splunk On-Call administrators who still operate VictorOps-era routing keys alongside modern Splunk workflows. This use case is deliberately about the human paging lifecycle after an alert becomes an incident: creation timestamps, acknowledgement timestamps, resolution timestamps, escalation ladder depth, and cross-tool identity of the same fault when Splunk saved searches, Enterprise Security notables, or ITSI episodes coexist with vendor paging timelines. UC-13.3.5 remains the home for alert storm economics, duplicate fingerprints per minute, and notification fan-out volume rather than per-incident acknowledgement interval service levels and mean time to resolve trending. UC-13.3.1 remains legacy Nagios, Zabbix, and SCOM ingest reconciliation rather than SaaS paging vendor APIs. UC-13.3.2 remains Prometheus scrape and federation integrity rather than on-call acknowledgement math. UC-13.3.7 remains distributed trace sampling coverage rather than paging acknowledgement percentiles. UC-13.3.15 remains OpenTelemetry Collector backpressure rather than escalation policy effectiveness. This document answers whether responders meet acknowledgement promises, how long incidents stay open by urgency and service, which services generate the most paging debt, how often incidents self-heal before a human acknowledges, and which tool should win when Splunk, PagerDuty, and ITSI disagree on the same outage window.

PagerDuty exposes incidents through REST list and show APIs plus webhooks for event-driven mirrors. Normalized fields include incident id, service name, urgency, priority, status transitions, assignee and acknowledger identities, escalation policy identifiers, and optional tags that tie back to change tickets or customers. Opsgenie exposes alerts and audit activity through REST with priorities that map to internal urgency vocabulary; notification streams may land as separate sourcetypes when you split outbound SMS or voice attempts from core alert objects. Splunk On-Call and legacy VictorOps exports include routing keys, entity names, escalation timelines, and acknowledgement user handles that must align to corporate directory email for roster joins. Splunk-native alert traffic accelerated under the CIM Alerts data model supplies upstream cause signals keyed by signature and dest that you compare to paging vendor incident rows when deciding source of truth during investigations.

RBAC and credential hygiene are mandatory. Read-only API tokens should poll vendor REST endpoints or receive webhooks on hardened collectors without interactive admin passwords on laptops. Store Splunk HTTP Event Collector tokens and modular input secrets in vault-backed credential stores with rotation contracts. Webhook receivers must validate shared secrets or signature headers so a single compromised VLAN cannot forge incidents. Legal reviewers should confirm customer identifiers in incident titles meet retention and minimization policies before landing raw payloads in shared indexes. Directory teams should approve inclusion of assignee_email and responder_email fields in Splunk because they are personally identifiable.

Index design: designate index=observability for primary vendor JSON lanes when you want a single analyst namespace, and index=pagerduty or index=opsgenie when compliance requires segregated retention. Hot retention should cover at least fourteen days for acknowledgement replay, thirty days for quarterly MTTA and MTTR reviews, and cold storage aligned to observability governance. Stamp change_ticket_id and drill_id on forwarded payloads whenever reliability teams run game days so downstream severity logic can downgrade expected noise. Separate lab stacks into non-production indexes excluded from executive dashboards.

Field normalization: coalesce vendor-specific identifiers into incident_id and incident_number columns that survive API version upgrades. Map created_at, acknowledged_at, and resolved_at into ISO-8601 strings before strptime in SPL. Normalize service_name with lowercase trim and inventory-backed aliases so checkout-api and Checkout-API collapse correctly. Map heterogeneous priority strings into urgency and priority columns for case() tiering without brittle string equality. Preserve tool labels per multisearch arm so reconciliation logic never confuses which vendor spoke last. Derive escalation_level integers from vendor fields that describe how many ladder rungs fired or how many human handoffs occurred.

Risk briefing: mean acknowledgement time can look worse than reality when webhooks arrive late or when Splunk indexing lag exceeds vendor UI clocks; always compare _time to vendor-reported epoch fields before blaming people. Mean time to resolve can be skewed by incidents left open for paperwork after service recovery; pair operational resolve times with business closure processes. Acknowledgement percentiles highlight overloaded rotations but may shame individuals if exported without management context; restrict dashboards to aggregated service views in human resources sensitive environments. Cross-tool source-of-truth hints are advisory: legal incidents may still require PagerDuty as record even when Splunk fired first.

Collector posture: modular inputs that poll REST should use courteous intervals and exponential backoff on 429 responses. Webhook receivers should deduplicate by delivery id headers when vendors retry. When Splunk shows gaps while vendor UIs show continuity, verify indexer connectivity, token scopes, and daylight saving configuration on pollers.

Differentiation recap: paging vendor incident lifecycle, acknowledgement SLA intervals, resolve intervals, escalation depth analytics, noisy service ranking, auto-resolve detection, and Splunk alert upstream correlation, not legacy NMS syslog reconciliation alone, not Prometheus scrape health alone, not alert storm rate per minute in isolation, not trace sampling ratios, not collector queue occupancy narratives.

Step 2 — Configure data collection

Lane A lands PagerDuty incidents and webhook mirrors with sourcetype=pagerduty:incident for REST-style snapshots and sourcetype=pagerduty:webhook for v3-style event narratives when your add-on splits them. Install or reference the Splunk Add-on for PagerDuty patterns on heavy forwarders or search tier inputs that can poll incidents or receive HTTPS webhooks according to your architecture. Preserve incident_id, service_name, urgency, priority, assignee_email, responder_email, escalation_policy, escalation_level, tags, created_at, acknowledged_at, and resolved_at extractions. Align clocks so Splunk _time reflects vendor creation time rather than indexer arrival when possible.

Lane B captures Opsgenie with sourcetype=opsgenie:alert for alert API objects and sourcetype=opsgenie:notification for outbound notification attempts when your integration separates them for deliverability forensics. Map alertId, tinyId, entity, team, priority, owner, createdAt, acknowledgedAt, closedAt, and escalation metadata. Volume-control high-churn development teams by routing lab workspaces to sandbox indexes.

Lane C forwards Splunk On-Call and VictorOps incident exports with sourcetype=splunk_oncall:incident or sourcetype=victorops:incident depending on exporter version. Retain routing_key, entity_name, start_time, ack_time, end_time, ack_user, and escalation_policy fields. When migrating from VictorOps branding to Splunk On-Call, keep parallel sourcetype tags until historical comparisons finish.

Lane D reads accelerated CIM Alerts summaries via tstats in the primary SPL arm keyed on Alerts.signature and Alerts.dest so Splunk-native detections appear beside paging rows for reconciliation. Ensure datamodel=Alerts acceleration is healthy and that signature maps to saved search or correlation search names your teams recognize during bridges.

Maintain oncall_responder_shift_roster.csv with columns responder_email, shift_bucket, on_call_tier, and expected_ack_sla_min refreshed nightly from directory and scheduling exports. Include a synthetic unassigned_responder row so empty assignees still join predictably. Maintain escalation_policy_ground_truth.csv with columns escalation_policy, expected_primary_minutes, max_escalation_levels, and policy_owner refreshed from PagerDuty and Opsgenie policy exports after every policy change window.

props.conf should set KV_MODE=json for vendor JSON, TIMESTAMP_FIELDS when HEC stamps authoritative time, and TRUNCATE high enough for long incident bodies. Use FIELDALIAS to align assignee and owner variants.

Example curl helpers for design reviews from bastion hosts with credentials:

```bash
curl -fsS -H "Authorization: Token token=REDACTED" "https://api.pagerduty.com/incidents?limit=5"
curl -fsS -H "Authorization: GenieKey REDACTED" "https://api.opsgenie.com/v2/alerts?limit=5"
```

Pre-save validation: each sourcetype must return non-zero events in a one-hour steady-state window before enabling the scheduled search. Confirm webhook and REST lanes do not double-count identical incident ids without dedupe keys.

Dashboards: operational dashboards should expose MTTA and MTTR trend lines by service_name, acknowledgement P95 by rotation, escalation depth histograms, top noisy services, auto-resolve ratio tiles, and drilldowns to raw JSON.

Step 3 — Create the search and alert

Save the SPL as observability_uc_13_3_3_oncall_mtta_mttr_reconciliation with a fifteen-minute schedule during production steady state, dispatch.earliest_time=-24h@h, dispatch.latest_time=now, and alert when recommended_action implies roster or escalation review for executive services. Throttle duplicate service_name rows for thirty minutes unless acknowledgement P95 worsens by more than ten minutes. Include recommended_action, sot, and noisy_rank in pager descriptions so incident commanders open human-runbooks without re-deriving joins during bridges.

Pipeline narrative distinct from UC-13.3.5: this multisearch unifies four lanes—PagerDuty, Opsgenie, Splunk On-Call or VictorOps, and Splunk-native CIM Alerts—so you can compute service-level acknowledgement and resolve intervals, highlight the slowest five percent of acknowledgement times through perc95 aggregates, count escalations past primary coverage using escalation_level compared to ground-truth lookups, rank noisy services by incident volume, flag auto-resolved incidents that clear before acknowledgement, and emit source-of-truth hints when recent Alerts.dest activity aligns with paging vendor rows. coalesce() ladders absorb timestamp format drift. streamstats provides short-window stability checks on acknowledgement sequences per service and tool. eventstats adds per-service volume and perc95 context before roster and policy joins. join with inputlookup subsearches enriches expected SLA minutes from escalation_policy_ground_truth.csv and shift metadata from oncall_responder_shift_roster.csv. A secondary tstats join adds recent Alerts.dest activity for reconciliation hints. case() encodes severity tiers from acknowledgement SLA breaches, escalation load, and priority. The closing stats aggregate produces executive-ready rows with explicit noisy_rank ordering.

Fenced SPL must match the spl JSON field exactly:

```spl
`comment("UC-13.3.3 on-call MTTA MTTR multisearch PagerDuty Opsgenie VictorOps SplunkOnCall Splunk alerts roster policy tstats earliest=-24h@h")`
| multisearch
    [ search (index=observability OR index=pagerduty) (sourcetype=pagerduty:incident OR sourcetype=pagerduty:webhook) earliest=-24h@h latest=now
      | eval tool="pagerduty"
      | eval incident_id=trim(toString(coalesce(id, incident_id, "")))
      | eval incident_number=trim(toString(coalesce(incident_number, number, "")))
      | eval service_name=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(service_name, service, summary, ""))))
      | eval urgency=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(urgency, ""))))
      | eval priority=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(priority, urgency, ""))))
      | eval created_at=trim(toString(coalesce(created_at, created, "")))
      | eval acknowledged_at=trim(toString(coalesce(acknowledged_at, last_status_change_at, "")))
      | eval resolved_at=trim(toString(coalesce(resolved_at, "")))
      | eval assignee_email=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(assignee_email, "", ""))))
      | eval responder_email=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(responder_email, acknowledger, ""))))
      | eval escalation_policy=trim(toString(coalesce(escalation_policy, escalation_policy_id, "")))
      | eval escalation_level=tonumber(coalesce(escalation_level, number_of_escalations, "1"))
      | eval tags=trim(toString(coalesce(tags, "", "")))
      | fields _time tool incident_id incident_number service_name urgency priority created_at acknowledged_at resolved_at assignee_email responder_email escalation_policy escalation_level tags ]
    [ search (index=observability OR index=opsgenie) (sourcetype=opsgenie:alert OR sourcetype=opsgenie:notification) earliest=-24h@h latest=now
      | eval tool="opsgenie"
      | eval incident_id=trim(toString(coalesce(alertId, tinyId, id, alias, "")))
      | eval incident_number=incident_id
      | eval service_name=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(entity, team, team_name, service, ""))))
      | eval urgency=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(priority, ""))))
      | eval priority=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(priority, ""))))
      | eval created_at=trim(toString(coalesce(createdAt, created_at, "")))
      | eval acknowledged_at=trim(toString(coalesce(acknowledgedAt, acknowledged_at, "")))
      | eval resolved_at=trim(toString(coalesce(closedAt, resolved_at, "")))
      | eval assignee_email=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(owner, "", ""))))
      | eval responder_email=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(responder_email, "", ""))))
      | eval escalation_policy=trim(toString(coalesce(escalation, policy, "")))
      | eval escalation_level=tonumber(coalesce(level, escalation_level, "1"))
      | eval tags=trim(toString(coalesce(tags, details, "")))
      | fields _time tool incident_id incident_number service_name urgency priority created_at acknowledged_at resolved_at assignee_email responder_email escalation_policy escalation_level tags ]
    [ search (index=observability OR index=victorops) (sourcetype=splunk_oncall:incident OR sourcetype=victorops:incident) earliest=-24h@h latest=now
      | eval tool="splunk_oncall"
      | eval incident_id=trim(toString(coalesce(incident_id, incidentId, "")))
      | eval incident_number=trim(toString(coalesce(incident_number, incidentNumber, "")))
      | eval service_name=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(service_name, entity_name, routing_key, ""))))
      | eval urgency=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(urgency, ""))))
      | eval priority=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(priority, urgency, ""))))
      | eval created_at=trim(toString(coalesce(created_at, start_time, "")))
      | eval acknowledged_at=trim(toString(coalesce(acknowledged_at, ack_time, "")))
      | eval resolved_at=trim(toString(coalesce(resolved_at, end_time, "")))
      | eval assignee_email=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(assignee_email, "", ""))))
      | eval responder_email=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(responder_email, ack_user, ""))))
      | eval escalation_policy=trim(toString(coalesce(escalation_policy, "")))
      | eval escalation_level=tonumber(coalesce(escalation_level, "1"))
      | eval tags=trim(toString(coalesce(tags, "", "")))
      | fields _time tool incident_id incident_number service_name urgency priority created_at acknowledged_at resolved_at assignee_email responder_email escalation_policy escalation_level tags ]
    [ | tstats latest(_time) AS _time latest(Alerts.severity) AS alert_sev FROM datamodel=Alerts WHERE nodename=Alerts earliest=-24h@h latest=now BY Alerts.signature Alerts.dest
      | rename Alerts.signature AS incident_id Alerts.dest AS service_name
      | eval tool="splunk_native_alert"
      | eval service_name=lower(trim(toString(service_name)))
      | eval incident_number=incident_id
      | eval urgency="not_applicable"
      | eval priority=lower(trim(toString(alert_sev)))
      | eval created_at=strftime(_time, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
      | eval acknowledged_at=""
      | eval resolved_at=""
      | eval assignee_email=""
      | eval responder_email=""
      | eval escalation_policy="splunk_savedsearch"
      | eval escalation_level=0
      | eval tags="upstream_splunk_alert_lane"
      | fields _time tool incident_id incident_number service_name urgency priority created_at acknowledged_at resolved_at assignee_email responder_email escalation_policy escalation_level tags ]
| eval created_epoch=coalesce(strptime(created_at, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"), strptime(created_at, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%z"), _time)
| eval ack_epoch=strptime(acknowledged_at, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
| eval resolved_epoch=strptime(resolved_at, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
| eval ack_min=if(isnotnull(ack_epoch) AND isnotnull(created_epoch), round((ack_epoch-created_epoch)/60,2), null())
| eval mttr_min=if(isnotnull(resolved_epoch) AND isnotnull(created_epoch), round((resolved_epoch-created_epoch)/60,2), null())
| eval auto_resolved=if(isnotnull(resolved_epoch) AND (isnull(ack_epoch) OR resolved_epoch<ack_epoch),1,0)
| eval escalated_past_primary=if(escalation_level>1,1,0)
| where len(service_name)>0
| fillnull value="unassigned_responder" responder_email
| fillnull value="default_policy" escalation_policy
| eval responder_email=lower(trim(toString(responder_email)))
| eventstats count AS svc_volume BY service_name
| eventstats perc95(ack_min) AS ack_p95_svc BY service_name
| sort 0 service_name tool - _time
| streamstats window=3 global=f current=f last(ack_min) AS prev_ack BY service_name tool
| join type=left max=0 escalation_policy
    [| inputlookup escalation_policy_ground_truth.csv
     | fields escalation_policy expected_primary_minutes max_escalation_levels policy_owner ]
| join type=left max=0 responder_email
    [| inputlookup oncall_responder_shift_roster.csv
     | eval responder_email=lower(trim(toString(responder_email)))
     | fields responder_email shift_bucket on_call_tier expected_ack_sla_min ]
| join type=left max=0 service_name
    [| tstats latest(_time) AS itsi_last FROM datamodel=Alerts WHERE nodename=Alerts earliest=-168h@h latest=now BY Alerts.dest
     | rename Alerts.dest AS service_name
     | eval service_name=lower(trim(toString(service_name))) ]
| eval severity_tier=case(auto_resolved==1 AND isnotnull(mttr_min) AND mttr_min<20,"info_autorecover", ack_min>coalesce(expected_primary_minutes,expected_ack_sla_min,30)*1.5 OR ack_p95_svc>45,"page_ack_sla", escalated_past_primary==1,"warn_escalation_load", match(priority,"p1|high|critical"),"warn_priority", true(),"info_steady")
| eval sot_hint=case(tool=="pagerduty" AND isnotnull(itsi_last),"pd_primary_with_recent_cim_alert_activity", tool=="opsgenie" AND isnotnull(itsi_last),"og_correlate_splunk_alert_dest", tool=="splunk_native_alert","treat_splunk_alert_as_upstream_compare_to_paging_timelines", true(),"paging_tool_native_timeline")
| stats avg(ack_min) AS mtta_min avg(mttr_min) AS mttr_min max(ack_p95_svc) AS ack_p95_min sum(auto_resolved) AS auto_resolved_cnt count AS incidents dc(tool) AS tools_touching sum(eval(if(tool=="splunk_native_alert",0,escalated_past_primary))) AS escalation_events sum(eval(if(tool=="splunk_native_alert",1,0))) AS splunk_alert_upstreams values(severity_tier) AS tier_samples values(sot_hint) AS sot values(tags) AS tag_samples values(urgency) AS urgency_samples BY service_name
| eval recommended_action=case(ack_p95_min>45 OR mtta_min>30,"review_responder_roster_sleep_pager_and_primary_coverage", escalation_events>=3,"tune_escalation_policy_reduce_manager_wakeups", splunk_alert_upstreams>12,"reconcile_splunk_notable_itsi_episodes_against_pagerduty_ids", true(),"continue_mtta_mttr_weekly_trend_review")
| sort 0 - incidents
| streamstats count AS noisy_rank
| table service_name mtta_min mttr_min ack_p95_min incidents auto_resolved_cnt escalation_events splunk_alert_upstreams tools_touching noisy_rank tier_samples sot urgency_samples recommended_action
```

savedsearches.conf sketch:

```ini
[observability_uc_13_3_3_oncall_mtta_mttr_reconciliation_alert]
cron_schedule = */15 * * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h@h
dispatch.latest_time = now
enableSched = 1
action.email = 1
action.email.to = platform-reliability-oncall@example.com
action.email.subject = On-call SLA $result.service_name$ rank=$result.noisy_rank$
counttype = number of events
relation = greater than
quantity = 0
search = | savedsearch observability_uc_13_3_3_oncall_mtta_mttr_reconciliation | where match(recommended_action,"review_|tune_")
```

Performance: if Job Inspector shows multisearch queue time above internal objectives, summarize vendor lanes hourly into a summary index keyed on service_name and tool, widen alert searches to earliest=-6h@h on summaries, and retain this full search for investigations.

Step 4 — Validate

Ground truth begins in PagerDuty incident timelines: compare created, acknowledged, and resolved timestamps for a sampled incident_id against Splunk parsed created_at, acknowledged_at, and resolved_at within one polling interval. When they disagree, verify API pagination gaps, webhook retries, indexer clock skew, and duplicate HEC submissions before blaming analytics.

Opsgenie validation: open alert details for the same tinyId or alias and confirm priority maps to Splunk priority fields. Splunk On-Call validation: compare routing key and entity timeline to Splunk rows for the same incident_number.

Cross-tool reconciliation: pick a row where sot hints reference both paging vendor and Splunk activity, then confirm Alerts.signature in Splunk matches the saved search or correlation search that should have fired first. When ITSI episodes are in scope for your deployment, compare episode start times to paging creation times without merging UC-13.3.5 storm logic into this control.

Percentile validation: export acknowledgement durations for a single service from the vendor UI if available and compare to Splunk perc95 with the same calendar window.

Synthetic drill: create a non-customer-impact test incident in a lab service, acknowledge within policy, resolve cleanly, confirm observability_uc_13_3_3_oncall_mtta_mttr_reconciliation returns a row with finite mtta_min and mttr_min.

Negative test: after quiet weekend hours with zero paging, filtering to review-tier recommended_action should return zero rows for thirty consecutive runs unless governance thresholds are miscalibrated.

Step 5 — Operationalize & Troubleshoot

Case 1 — Primary responder chronically misses first page within policy: review shift handoffs in oncall_responder_shift_roster.csv, validate phone push permissions, and inspect sleep pager health before escalating to individuals.

Case 2 — Escalation_policy_ground_truth.csv disagrees with live PagerDuty policy after a reorganization: refresh lookup from exported JSON, replay a tabletop drill, and update expected_primary_minutes with change ticket references.

Case 3 — Webhook-only ingestion stops while REST poller continues: deduplicate conflicting timelines using incident_id precedence rules documented in your runbook, then repair webhook TLS or firewall paths.

Case 4 — Opsgenie priority strings change after a template migration: update case() mappings for warn_priority without muting genuine high-urgency rows.

Case 5 — Splunk On-Call routing key renames fragment service_name: rebuild inventory aliases and normalize routing_key before ranking noisy services.

Case 6 — Auto-resolved incidents spike after a deployment adds resilient retries: celebrate recovery automation but confirm customer impact metrics still align; tune info_autorecover thresholds if noise masks genuine staffing gaps.

Case 7 — Acknowledgement P95 blows out during a single mega-incident: annotate bridge timelines, exclude change-window test traffic if tagged, and compare against UC-13.3.5 only for volume context without merging storm suppression into this SLA search.

Case 8 — CIM Alerts acceleration lag makes itsi_last look stale while paging screams: repair data model acceleration, then re-run reconciliation before declaring Splunk silent.

Case 9 — Duplicate incident rows from REST plus webhook double incident counts: implement dedupe on incident_id at ingest or add pre-stats filtering with vendor delivery ids.

Case 10 — GDPR-sensitive titles appear in executive dashboards: redact display fields in presentation layers while retaining hashed correlation keys in restricted indexes.

Case 11 — Manager escalation fatigue with high escalation_events: rebalance on-call depth, add secondary skill pools, and pair with executive-approved policy changes rather than muting root causes.

Case 12 — Tool selection disputes during audits: document that PagerDuty remains operational record for customer-facing incidents while Splunk remains analytical home for cross-domain correlation, using sot hints as guidance not legal attestation.

Closing checklist: five em-dash step headers are present; Step 3 fenced SPL matches the spl field exactly; multisearch lists four arms; coalesce, streamstats, eventstats, case, join, inputlookup, and tstats appear; final table lists fourteen analyst-visible columns including noisy_rank and splunk_alert_upstreams for cross-tool transparency.
spl:
`comment("UC-13.3.3 on-call MTTA MTTR multisearch PagerDuty Opsgenie VictorOps SplunkOnCall Splunk alerts roster policy tstats earliest=-24h@h")`
| multisearch
    [ search (index=observability OR index=pagerduty) (sourcetype=pagerduty:incident OR sourcetype=pagerduty:webhook) earliest=-24h@h latest=now
      | eval tool="pagerduty"
      | eval incident_id=trim(toString(coalesce(id, incident_id, "")))
      | eval incident_number=trim(toString(coalesce(incident_number, number, "")))
      | eval service_name=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(service_name, service, summary, ""))))
      | eval urgency=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(urgency, ""))))
      | eval priority=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(priority, urgency, ""))))
      | eval created_at=trim(toString(coalesce(created_at, created, "")))
      | eval acknowledged_at=trim(toString(coalesce(acknowledged_at, last_status_change_at, "")))
      | eval resolved_at=trim(toString(coalesce(resolved_at, "")))
      | eval assignee_email=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(assignee_email, "", ""))))
      | eval responder_email=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(responder_email, acknowledger, ""))))
      | eval escalation_policy=trim(toString(coalesce(escalation_policy, escalation_policy_id, "")))
      | eval escalation_level=tonumber(coalesce(escalation_level, number_of_escalations, "1"))
      | eval tags=trim(toString(coalesce(tags, "", "")))
      | fields _time tool incident_id incident_number service_name urgency priority created_at acknowledged_at resolved_at assignee_email responder_email escalation_policy escalation_level tags ]
    [ search (index=observability OR index=opsgenie) (sourcetype=opsgenie:alert OR sourcetype=opsgenie:notification) earliest=-24h@h latest=now
      | eval tool="opsgenie"
      | eval incident_id=trim(toString(coalesce(alertId, tinyId, id, alias, "")))
      | eval incident_number=incident_id
      | eval service_name=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(entity, team, team_name, service, ""))))
      | eval urgency=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(priority, ""))))
      | eval priority=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(priority, ""))))
      | eval created_at=trim(toString(coalesce(createdAt, created_at, "")))
      | eval acknowledged_at=trim(toString(coalesce(acknowledgedAt, acknowledged_at, "")))
      | eval resolved_at=trim(toString(coalesce(closedAt, resolved_at, "")))
      | eval assignee_email=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(owner, "", ""))))
      | eval responder_email=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(responder_email, "", ""))))
      | eval escalation_policy=trim(toString(coalesce(escalation, policy, "")))
      | eval escalation_level=tonumber(coalesce(level, escalation_level, "1"))
      | eval tags=trim(toString(coalesce(tags, details, "")))
      | fields _time tool incident_id incident_number service_name urgency priority created_at acknowledged_at resolved_at assignee_email responder_email escalation_policy escalation_level tags ]
    [ search (index=observability OR index=victorops) (sourcetype=splunk_oncall:incident OR sourcetype=victorops:incident) earliest=-24h@h latest=now
      | eval tool="splunk_oncall"
      | eval incident_id=trim(toString(coalesce(incident_id, incidentId, "")))
      | eval incident_number=trim(toString(coalesce(incident_number, incidentNumber, "")))
      | eval service_name=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(service_name, entity_name, routing_key, ""))))
      | eval urgency=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(urgency, ""))))
      | eval priority=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(priority, urgency, ""))))
      | eval created_at=trim(toString(coalesce(created_at, start_time, "")))
      | eval acknowledged_at=trim(toString(coalesce(acknowledged_at, ack_time, "")))
      | eval resolved_at=trim(toString(coalesce(resolved_at, end_time, "")))
      | eval assignee_email=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(assignee_email, "", ""))))
      | eval responder_email=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(responder_email, ack_user, ""))))
      | eval escalation_policy=trim(toString(coalesce(escalation_policy, "")))
      | eval escalation_level=tonumber(coalesce(escalation_level, "1"))
      | eval tags=trim(toString(coalesce(tags, "", "")))
      | fields _time tool incident_id incident_number service_name urgency priority created_at acknowledged_at resolved_at assignee_email responder_email escalation_policy escalation_level tags ]
    [ | tstats latest(_time) AS _time latest(Alerts.severity) AS alert_sev FROM datamodel=Alerts WHERE nodename=Alerts earliest=-24h@h latest=now BY Alerts.signature Alerts.dest
      | rename Alerts.signature AS incident_id Alerts.dest AS service_name
      | eval tool="splunk_native_alert"
      | eval service_name=lower(trim(toString(service_name)))
      | eval incident_number=incident_id
      | eval urgency="not_applicable"
      | eval priority=lower(trim(toString(alert_sev)))
      | eval created_at=strftime(_time, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
      | eval acknowledged_at=""
      | eval resolved_at=""
      | eval assignee_email=""
      | eval responder_email=""
      | eval escalation_policy="splunk_savedsearch"
      | eval escalation_level=0
      | eval tags="upstream_splunk_alert_lane"
      | fields _time tool incident_id incident_number service_name urgency priority created_at acknowledged_at resolved_at assignee_email responder_email escalation_policy escalation_level tags ]
| eval created_epoch=coalesce(strptime(created_at, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"), strptime(created_at, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%z"), _time)
| eval ack_epoch=strptime(acknowledged_at, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
| eval resolved_epoch=strptime(resolved_at, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
| eval ack_min=if(isnotnull(ack_epoch) AND isnotnull(created_epoch), round((ack_epoch-created_epoch)/60,2), null())
| eval mttr_min=if(isnotnull(resolved_epoch) AND isnotnull(created_epoch), round((resolved_epoch-created_epoch)/60,2), null())
| eval auto_resolved=if(isnotnull(resolved_epoch) AND (isnull(ack_epoch) OR resolved_epoch<ack_epoch),1,0)
| eval escalated_past_primary=if(escalation_level>1,1,0)
| where len(service_name)>0
| fillnull value="unassigned_responder" responder_email
| fillnull value="default_policy" escalation_policy
| eval responder_email=lower(trim(toString(responder_email)))
| eventstats count AS svc_volume BY service_name
| eventstats perc95(ack_min) AS ack_p95_svc BY service_name
| sort 0 service_name tool - _time
| streamstats window=3 global=f current=f last(ack_min) AS prev_ack BY service_name tool
| join type=left max=0 escalation_policy
    [| inputlookup escalation_policy_ground_truth.csv
     | fields escalation_policy expected_primary_minutes max_escalation_levels policy_owner ]
| join type=left max=0 responder_email
    [| inputlookup oncall_responder_shift_roster.csv
     | eval responder_email=lower(trim(toString(responder_email)))
     | fields responder_email shift_bucket on_call_tier expected_ack_sla_min ]
| join type=left max=0 service_name
    [| tstats latest(_time) AS itsi_last FROM datamodel=Alerts WHERE nodename=Alerts earliest=-168h@h latest=now BY Alerts.dest
     | rename Alerts.dest AS service_name
     | eval service_name=lower(trim(toString(service_name))) ]
| eval severity_tier=case(auto_resolved==1 AND isnotnull(mttr_min) AND mttr_min<20,"info_autorecover", ack_min>coalesce(expected_primary_minutes,expected_ack_sla_min,30)*1.5 OR ack_p95_svc>45,"page_ack_sla", escalated_past_primary==1,"warn_escalation_load", match(priority,"p1|high|critical"),"warn_priority", true(),"info_steady")
| eval sot_hint=case(tool=="pagerduty" AND isnotnull(itsi_last),"pd_primary_with_recent_cim_alert_activity", tool=="opsgenie" AND isnotnull(itsi_last),"og_correlate_splunk_alert_dest", tool=="splunk_native_alert","treat_splunk_alert_as_upstream_compare_to_paging_timelines", true(),"paging_tool_native_timeline")
| stats avg(ack_min) AS mtta_min avg(mttr_min) AS mttr_min max(ack_p95_svc) AS ack_p95_min sum(auto_resolved) AS auto_resolved_cnt count AS incidents dc(tool) AS tools_touching sum(eval(if(tool=="splunk_native_alert",0,escalated_past_primary))) AS escalation_events sum(eval(if(tool=="splunk_native_alert",1,0))) AS splunk_alert_upstreams values(severity_tier) AS tier_samples values(sot_hint) AS sot values(tags) AS tag_samples values(urgency) AS urgency_samples BY service_name
| eval recommended_action=case(ack_p95_min>45 OR mtta_min>30,"review_responder_roster_sleep_pager_and_primary_coverage", escalation_events>=3,"tune_escalation_policy_reduce_manager_wakeups", splunk_alert_upstreams>12,"reconcile_splunk_notable_itsi_episodes_against_pagerduty_ids", true(),"continue_mtta_mttr_weekly_trend_review")
| sort 0 - incidents
| streamstats count AS noisy_rank
| table service_name mtta_min mttr_min ack_p95_min incidents auto_resolved_cnt escalation_events splunk_alert_upstreams tools_touching noisy_rank tier_samples sot urgency_samples recommended_action
cimSpl:
| tstats summariesonly=true latest(Alerts.severity) AS sev latest(Alerts.signature) AS sig count FROM datamodel=Alerts WHERE nodename=Alerts earliest=-24h@h latest=now BY Alerts.dest
| rename Alerts.dest AS alert_service
| join type=left max=0 alert_service [| inputlookup escalation_policy_ground_truth.csv | eval alert_service=lower(trim(service_catalog_key)) | fields alert_service policy_owner expected_primary_minutes]
| table alert_service sev sig count policy_owner expected_primary_minutes
dataModelAcceleration: Accelerate Splunk CIM Alerts summaries for signature, severity, and dest fields that map from Splunk saved searches, correlation searches, and vendor mirrors; retain thirty days for quarterly acknowledgement and resolve attestations.
visualization: Service-level table with mtta_min, mttr_min, ack_p95_min, noisy_rank, splunk_alert_upstreams, tier_samples, and sot drilldowns to raw pagerduty, opsgenie, splunk_oncall, and victorops events; line charts for weekly MTTA and MTTR trends; single-value tiles for auto_resolved_cnt ratio and escalation_events concentration.

references.title: Splunk Docs — Use the Common Information Model Add-on

knownFalsePositives: Planned game-day drills and controlled failover exercises intentionally stretch acknowledgement intervals; tag drill metadata in vendor custom fields or lookup tables before paging people for SLA breaches. Vendor API pagination or rate-limit backoff can delay incident rows until minutes after the UI shows action, inflating acknowledgement math until pollers catch up; compare vendor epoch fields to Splunk _time. Webhook retry storms can duplicate incident_id rows and inflate incident counts until dedupe keys land at ingest. Shift-boundary handoffs near midnight local time split acknowledgement ownership between roster rows; widen join keys with shift_bucket overlap rules rather than blaming individuals. Opsgenie notification-only sourcetypes without core alert bodies can lack resolved_at until the alert arm arrives; require paired sourcetypes before declaring auto-resolve surges. Splunk CIM Alerts rows without paging counterparts are analytical upstream signals, not proof that paging failed; treat splunk_alert_upstreams as reconciliation context. Maintenance annotations that suppress pages but leave Splunk alerts firing can make Splunk appear ahead of paging vendors without a real disagreement. Executive dashboards that average across globally distributed teams without region splits can misread fairness; segment by region or follow-the-sun labels. Policy exports that lag reorganizations make expected_primary_minutes stale; refresh escalation_policy_ground_truth.csv within one business day of policy edits.
hardware: Applies to SaaS paging and Splunk Cloud or Splunk Enterprise search tiers that receive HTTPS webhooks, REST poller modular inputs, and accelerated CIM Alerts summaries; no proprietary appliance is required beyond standard hardened forwarders or hosted inputs.
exclusions: Does not replace UC-13.3.5 alert storm rate and duplicate fingerprint economics, UC-13.3.1 legacy NMS ingest reconciliation, UC-13.3.2 Prometheus scrape health, UC-13.3.7 trace sampling coverage, UC-13.3.15 OpenTelemetry Collector backpressure, deep ITSI episode policy authoring, or automated muting of vendor incidents; it supplies lifecycle metrics and advisory source-of-truth hints for human decisions.

controlTest.positiveScenario: In a lab tenant, create a PagerDuty test incident on a non-customer service, acknowledge within policy, resolve with timestamps visible in the vendor UI, ingest parallel Opsgenie or Splunk On-Call test traffic if licensed, and confirm observability_uc_13_3_3_oncall_mtta_mttr_reconciliation returns finite mtta_min and mttr_min for the normalized service_name within two search windows.
controlTest.negativeScenario: After a quiet holiday weekend with zero legitimate pages and only heartbeat traffic, filtering recommended_action to roster or escalation review tiers should return zero rows for thirty consecutive scheduled runs when lookups and thresholds are calibrated.

evidence: Saved search observability_uc_13_3_3_oncall_mtta_mttr_reconciliation with fifteen-minute schedule; dashboard drilldowns on paging sourcetypes; weekly CSV exports of service-level MTTA, MTTR, and noisy_rank archived to a restricted index.
